questions.question: A new colleague needs to add other people to a workspace and reshare its items, but the security team insists that no one outside the original owners be able to delete the workspace or promote anyone to administrator. Which workspace role meets these requirements?

options.A: Member
options.B: Contributor
options.C: Admin
options.D: Viewer

questions.explanation: Member can add people to the Member, Contributor, and Viewer roles and can reshare items, but cannot add or remove admins or delete the workspace. Admin is too broad because it can delete the workspace and add other admins; Contributor can create and edit items but cannot grant access to others; Viewer is read-only.

questions.question: An operations engineer wants one screen that lists individual dataflow refreshes, pipeline runs, and Spark application executions across a workspace, each with its submitter, status, and duration. Which experience should they open?

options.A: The query insights views in the SQL analytics endpoint
options.B: The Monitoring hub
options.C: The Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app
options.D: The workspace lineage view

questions.explanation: The Monitoring hub aggregates per-item activity runs (refreshes, pipeline runs, Spark jobs, semantic model operations) with status, submitter, timestamps, and duration. The Capacity Metrics app reports capacity-level CU consumption rather than individual run history; query insights covers only warehouse/SQL endpoint queries; lineage shows item dependencies, not run history.

questions.question: Your tenant admin has enabled Git integration. Which source control services can a workspace be connected to directly, without a third-party bridge? Select all that apply.

options.A: A self-hosted Git server reached by HTTPS clone URL
options.B: GitHub
options.C: Bitbucket Cloud
options.D: Azure DevOps (Azure Repos)

questions.explanation: Fabric Git integration natively connects to Azure DevOps (Azure Repos) and GitHub only. GitLab, Bitbucket, and arbitrary self-hosted Git endpoints are not supported targets; pointing at a generic clone URL is not an available option in the Git connection dialog.

questions.question: A team is setting up a deployment pipeline for the first time and asks how the stages are arranged out of the box. What is the default configuration?

options.A: Two stages mapped to a Build workspace and a Release workspace
options.B: One shared stage that all assigned workspaces deploy into
options.C: Three stages named Development, Test, and Production, each bound to its own workspace
options.D: Four stages: Dev, QA, Staging, and Production

questions.explanation: A deployment pipeline starts with three stages (Development, Test, Production), and each stage is associated with a separate workspace through which content is promoted. Stages can be added, removed, or renamed afterward, but the default is three, not two or four, and they are not a single shared stage.

questions.question: Finance reports that a shared F64 capacity occasionally returns errors when users open reports, while scheduled refreshes seem to keep running. In the Capacity Metrics app the cumulative carry-forward on the Overages tab has been climbing for an hour. Which conclusion and first action are correct?

options.A: OneLake storage for the workspace is exhausted; run VACUUM on the largest Delta tables to free space
options.B: Surge protection rejected the interactive reports; raise the surge-protection recovery threshold to 100%
options.C: The capacity left its home region; reassign the affected workspaces to a capacity in the correct region
options.D: Smoothed usage crossed the 60-minute interactive threshold, so interactive requests are being rejected while background jobs still run; reschedule or optimize heavy workloads, or scale the SKU
options.E: The 24-hour background window was breached, so all jobs are being rejected; pause the capacity and resume it to clear the state

questions.explanation: Sustained CU usage above the F64 limit triggers progressive throttling (interactive delay then interactive rejection), which blocks interactive/report queries while background jobs continue; the fix is to reduce/reschedule heavy jobs or scale the capacity. Storage exhaustion or region issues do not produce throttling carry-forward, and background rejection would block scheduled refreshes rather than interactive queries.

questions.question: A user is added to a Lakehouse with the item-level Read permission only, and holds no workspace role. They can see the Lakehouse in their list and connect to its SQL analytics endpoint, but a Spark notebook reading the same tables through the OneLake path fails. What explains this?

options.A: Read grants discovery plus connect, but reading table data through OneLake APIs and Spark requires the ReadAll permission, which Read alone does not include
options.B: Read silently elevates the user to the workspace Viewer role, which blocks Spark access
options.C: Read includes ReadData and ReadAll, so the Spark failure must be an unrelated cluster error
options.D: Read permits writing table schema but not reading rows, so any read path fails until Write is removed

questions.explanation: Item Read lets a user discover and connect to the item; data access through the TDS/SQL endpoint comes via ReadData, while reading through OneLake APIs and Spark requires ReadAll. With only Read, the SQL endpoint connection can succeed but the OneLake/Spark path is denied. Read does not bundle ReadAll, does not change workspace roles, and is not a write grant.

questions.question: A platform team wants a service principal in their release pipeline to spin up a workspace, bind it to an existing capacity, and create a Lakehouse and a Notebook, with no portal clicks. Which approach fits?

options.A: Call the Power BI embedding REST endpoints, which now cover all Fabric item types
options.B: Call the Fabric REST APIs (Core workspace/role APIs plus the item-creation APIs) under api.fabric.microsoft.com
options.C: Drive the Azure Resource Manager APIs, since every Fabric item is an ARM resource
options.D: Use Microsoft Graph application APIs to provision the workspace and its items

questions.explanation: Workspace creation, capacity assignment, role assignments, and item creation (Lakehouse, Notebook) are exposed through the Fabric REST APIs under api.fabric.microsoft.com and support service-principal auth. ARM provisions the capacity resource itself but not Fabric items; the Power BI embedding APIs and Microsoft Graph do not create Fabric Lakehouses or Notebooks.

questions.question: A compliance team needs every report user to see only the sales rows for their own region, and they also need a few salary columns hidden from analysts, all enforced at the SQL analytics endpoint. Which TWO security features deliver these two requirements? Select all that apply.

options.A: A workspace Viewer assignment for the analysts
options.B: Column-level security (CLS) using GRANT/DENY on specific columns
options.C: OneLake data access roles scoped to a folder
options.D: Row-level security (RLS) defined with security predicates
options.E: Dynamic data masking applied to the salary columns

questions.explanation: RLS filters rows per user via security predicates, and CLS restricts which columns a user can select. Dynamic data masking only obfuscates displayed values (privileged users still see them and it is not true access control), OneLake data access roles scope file/folder access rather than columns, and a Viewer role does not implement either requirement. The two correct controls are RLS and CLS.

questions.question: After promoting from Test to Production in a deployment pipeline, a Production data pipeline still copies from the Test Lakehouse. The team wants the binding corrected automatically on every future deployment. What should they configure?

options.A: Turn on auto-binding by assigning the same capacity to the Test and Production workspaces
options.B: Parameterize the pipeline and edit the parameter value in Production after each deployment
options.C: Define deployment rules on the Production stage that rebind the data source to the Production Lakehouse
options.D: Switch the Production stage from deployment-pipeline promotion to direct Git sync so the connection follows the branch

questions.explanation: Deployment rules let you set stage-specific values, such as data source bindings, that are applied automatically during each deployment, so Production points at the Production source without manual edits. Git sync does not rebind data sources by stage, editing a parameter every time is still manual, and sharing a capacity has no effect on source bindings.

questions.question: Data engineers must be able to build and run notebooks, lakehouses, and pipelines in a workspace, but must not be able to change who has access or remove the workspace. Following least privilege, which role do you assign?

options.A: Member
options.B: Admin
options.C: Viewer
options.D: Contributor

questions.explanation: Contributor allows creating, editing, and executing items but cannot manage workspace access or delete the workspace, which is exactly the least-privilege fit. Member can additionally add people and reshare, Admin can delete the workspace and manage admins, and Viewer cannot create or run items at all.

questions.question: A governance lead wants a 'Confidential' classification applied to a Lakehouse to travel automatically to a semantic model built on it and to files exported from that model. Which capability provides this inheritance?

options.A: Deployment pipeline rules that carry metadata between stages
options.B: Tenant-level data loss prevention (DLP) policies for Fabric
options.C: OneLake data access roles propagated to child items
options.D: Microsoft Purview Information Protection sensitivity labels integrated with Fabric
options.E: Endorsement and promotion of the Lakehouse item

questions.explanation: Sensitivity labels from Microsoft Purview Information Protection are integrated with Fabric and inherit downstream, so a label on a source item flows to dependent items and exported content. OneLake data access roles control read access not classification, endorsement signals trust not sensitivity, deployment rules move content between stages, and DLP acts on labeled data rather than applying the label inheritance itself.

questions.question: Your team wants code review to gate everything reaching Production. The Dev workspace is connected to Azure DevOps. Which workflow best balances review with controlled promotion?

options.A: Connect the Production workspace to the main branch so merges land in Production directly
options.B: Have developers branch out from Dev, raise a pull request into main for review, then promote Dev to Test to Production through a deployment pipeline
options.C: Commit straight to main from Dev and rely on deployment rules to catch problems in Production
options.D: Keep one shared Dev workspace with no branches and require a manual sign-off before each promotion

questions.explanation: Branch-and-PR enforces review on main, and a deployment pipeline then handles staged promotion with stage-specific rules. Wiring Production to main bypasses staged promotion and review, a single shared workspace with no branching offers no isolation or review history, and committing straight to main skips review entirely.

questions.question: A Fabric Warehouse query that joins a large fact table to several dimensions scans far more data than expected. The team comes from SQL Server and proposes index changes. Which first action actually applies to Fabric Warehouse?

options.A: Create a clustered columnstore index with CREATE CLUSTERED COLUMNSTORE INDEX on the fact table
options.B: Add nonclustered rowstore indexes covering every join key
options.C: Ensure statistics are current and reshape the query to scan less data; consider data clustering on the common filter column
options.D: Switch the warehouse to Direct Lake mode to avoid scanning Parquet

questions.explanation: Fabric Warehouse stores data as columnstore Delta/Parquet and does not expose user-created indexes; tuning relies on accurate statistics, good query design, and (in preview) data clustering to reduce data scanned. Manual clustered/nonclustered index DDL is not supported, and Direct Lake is a semantic-model storage mode, not a warehouse query setting.

questions.question: A partner analyst needs to read exactly one table in a Lakehouse and nothing else, and must not be a workspace member. Which approaches can satisfy this with least privilege? Select all that apply.

options.A: Assign the analyst as a capacity contributor in the Azure portal
options.B: Share the item and grant SELECT on just that table/view at the SQL analytics endpoint
options.C: Share the item and use a OneLake data access role scoped to that table's folder
options.D: Add the analyst as a workspace Viewer so they inherit read on all items
options.E: Grant the analyst the Contributor role on the workspace

questions.explanation: Object-level SQL grants (GRANT SELECT on one table/view) and OneLake data access roles scoped to a single table's folder both achieve table-only access without workspace membership. Workspace Viewer grants read across all items, Contributor grants read/write across the workspace, and a capacity role governs the capacity, none of which is least-privilege table scoping.

questions.question: An auditor asks two things: which user triggered the most recent refresh of a specific semantic model, and whether any pipeline failed in the past day across the workspace. What is the single fastest place to answer both?

options.A: The workspace settings and access pages
options.B: The Capacity Metrics app, filtered to the workspace
options.C: DESCRIBE HISTORY on the underlying Delta tables
options.D: The Monitoring hub, filtered by item type and status

questions.explanation: The Monitoring hub lists activities with submitter, timestamp, status, and duration and supports filtering by item type and status, so both the refresh-submitter and failed-pipeline questions are answered in one place. The Capacity Metrics app shows CU consumption not run identities, DESCRIBE HISTORY shows Delta commits not refresh submitters or pipeline status, and settings pages show neither.

questions.question: A PySpark notebook job intermittently dies with executor out-of-memory errors. The Spark UI shows one stage of a join takes far longer than the others and processes most of the rows. Which TWO actions directly target the likely cause? Select all that apply.

options.A: Apply key salting to spread the hot key across more partitions
options.B: Rewrite the notebook in T-SQL against the SQL analytics endpoint
options.C: Coalesce the DataFrame to a single partition before the join
options.D: Disable Adaptive Query Execution to make partitioning deterministic
options.E: Broadcast the smaller dimension so the large table isn't shuffled on the skewed key

questions.explanation: A single long stage processing most rows indicates data skew on the join key; broadcasting the small side and salting the hot key are the standard mitigations. Coalescing to one partition concentrates data and worsens OOM, AQE actually helps skew so disabling it is counterproductive, and switching languages does not address the skew.

questions.question: In a Fabric Warehouse you need the ten most time-consuming completed queries over the last hour, using the built-in query insights views. Which statement returns them?

options.A: SELECT TOP 10 command, total_elapsed_time_ms FROM queryinsights.exec_requests_history WHERE start_time >= DATEADD(HOUR, -1, GETUTCDATE()) ORDER BY total_elapsed_time_ms DESC;
options.B: SELECT TOP 10 * FROM queryinsights.exec_sessions_history ORDER BY login_name;
options.C: SELECT TOP 10 * FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats ORDER BY total_worker_time DESC;
options.D: SELECT TOP 10 * FROM queryinsights.frequently_run_queries ORDER BY number_of_runs DESC;

questions.explanation: queryinsights.exec_requests_history holds per-request completed-query data including total_elapsed_time_ms, so filtering by start_time and ordering by elapsed time returns the slowest recent queries. sys.dm_exec_query_stats is a SQL Server DMV not used here, frequently_run_queries ranks by run count not duration, and exec_sessions_history is session-level, not per-query timing.

questions.question: You are upserting a micro-batch of changed rows into a Delta table in a Lakehouse, keyed on a business id, from a PySpark notebook. Which approach is correct and incremental?

options.A: spark.sql('UPDATE sales SET * FROM updates WHERE sales.id = updates.id')
options.B: DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'sales').alias('t').merge(updates.alias('s'), 't.id = s.id').whenMatchedUpdateAll().whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute()
options.C: updates.write.mode('overwrite').option('overwriteSchema', 'true').saveAsTable('sales')
options.D: updates.write.mode('append').saveAsTable('sales')  # rely on a later DISTINCT at read time

questions.explanation: Delta MERGE matches on the business key and updates matched rows while inserting unmatched ones atomically, which is the correct incremental upsert. Overwrite reloads the whole table rather than merging changes, the UPDATE ... SET * FROM syntax shown is not valid T-SQL/Spark SQL, and append-then-dedup produces duplicates and extra read cost.

questions.question: A Gold Delta table feeds a Power BI Direct Lake model and has accumulated many small files from frequent micro-batch writes. You want a one-time maintenance command that both compacts the files and applies the layout that benefits the Direct Lake read engine. Which command do you run in a notebook?

options.A: ALTER TABLE gold.sales SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true')
options.B: VACUUM gold.sales RETAIN 0 HOURS
options.C: OPTIMIZE gold.sales VORDER
options.D: OPTIMIZE gold.sales ZORDER BY (customer_id)

questions.explanation: OPTIMIZE performs the bin compaction and the VORDER clause applies V-Order, the write-time layout that most benefits Verti-Scan engines like Power BI Direct Lake; this is the correct one-time maintenance command. VACUUM only removes dereferenced files (and 0-hour retention is unsafe), ZORDER improves selective filtering but is not the Direct Lake read optimization asked for, and setting optimizeWrite only affects future writes rather than compacting existing files.

questions.question: Which tool surfaces capacity-unit consumption over time, the interactive-delay and rejection throttling charts, and the carry-forward overage on a Fabric capacity?

options.A: The Monitoring hub
options.B: Azure Monitor metrics for the capacity resource
options.C: The query insights sql_pool_insights view
options.D: The Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app

questions.explanation: The Capacity Metrics app is purpose-built for CU consumption, the throttling tabs (interactive delay, interactive rejection, background rejection), and the overages/carry-forward view. The Monitoring hub tracks per-item run activity, Azure Monitor does not expose Fabric's smoothing/throttling model, and sql_pool_insights covers warehouse pool pressure for queries rather than capacity-wide CU throttling.
